concept.name: TCP connection lifecycle
concept.definition: The fixed sequence of stages a TCP conversation between two computers passes through: an opening greeting where both sides agree to talk, a middle where data flows and arrivals are confirmed, and a closing goodbye so both sides know the conversation is over.

audience: a curious adult with no domain background
atomic_reason: The lifecycle only makes sense once you know that internet messages travel as small independent chunks, that the network may lose or jumble those chunks, and that TCP is one agreed rule-set for coping with that. Each of those ideas needs its own picture before the handshake, the data exchange, and the goodbye can click in one short figure.

prerequisites.name: Communication protocol
prerequisites.definition: An agreed set of rules two computers follow when talking, so each one knows what kind of message to expect next and what every message means.
prerequisites.why: TCP is one such rule-set, and its connection lifecycle is exactly the scripted order of messages that rule-set demands, so without the idea of computers following shared rules the stages look arbitrary.

prerequisites.name: Data packets
prerequisites.definition: Small labeled chunks that a message is chopped into before being sent across a network, each traveling on its own and carrying enough information to be put back in order.
prerequisites.why: Every stage of a TCP connection, including the opening greeting and the closing goodbye, is carried out by sending individual packets, so the reader must first picture data moving as separate chunks.

prerequisites.name: Best-effort delivery
prerequisites.definition: The way the internet moves packets with no promises: it tries to deliver each one, but packets can be lost, duplicated, delayed, or arrive out of order.
prerequisites.why: The handshake, the receipt confirmations, and the careful two-way goodbye exist only because the network itself guarantees nothing, so without this idea the whole lifecycle seems like pointless ceremony.
